steps.title: Identify what the conclusion requires
steps.content: The team concluded snakes transmit BovB to frogs via horizontal transfer (HT), either directly or indirectly — puzzling because frogs don't survive snake encounters. The conclusion needs support: evidence of a mechanism that could move BovB from snakes to frogs without requiring direct snake-frog gene exchange.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'BovB can be transmitted across frog species through HT.': This addresses frog-to-frog transfer, not snake-to-frog transfer — it does not explain how frogs first acquire BovB from snakes. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Parasites known to feed on species of snakes and frogs in which the BovB gene occurs also carry BovB.': Parasites that feed on both snakes and frogs while carrying BovB provide a biological vector for indirect transmission (snakes → parasites → frogs), solving the paradox of how frogs acquire the gene without surviving encounters. ✓
  • Choice C — 'BovB cannot be reliably transmitted from a snake species to bacteria that are usually encountered by frog species.': This rules out one indirect pathway (bacteria as vector), weakening rather than supporting the conclusion that indirect transmission is possible. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Frog species with BovB show few discernible advantages as compared with frog species that do not carry BovB.': This addresses fitness effects, not the transmission mechanism — it is irrelevant to how frogs acquire BovB from snakes. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
